body_html:
[Image: The Cascade Orienteering Club logo: a square containing the line-art outline of a mountain range. Overlaid on the mountains is a compass rose with arrows pointing N, S, E, W.]

Bearing 315

The newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club | November-December 1999
http://home1.gte.net/dnewel/Cascade/ | Page 1

WIOL Season Begins November 6

The 18th Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League season begins on November 6 with a meet at Marymoor Park in Redmond. You don't have to be a student to attend a WIOL meet – all meets have courses for the public as well. So come out, support our WIOL program, and have some fun, too.

We already have course setters for ALL the WIOL/Winter O’ series meets:

Meet Course Setter
WIOL 1: John Sincock
WIOL 2: Carl Moore
WIOL 3: Don Atkinson
WIOL 4: Paul Magnus
WIOL 5: Peter Golde
WIOL 6: Steve Miner
WIOL 7: Jim Siscel
WIOL Champs: Eric Bone

I’d like to thank all the above for stepping forward at the beginning of the season, and making my job as WIOL Director a lot less stressful. But we still have a lot of other jobs that need to be done on meet days–registration, start and finish timing, and marker pickup, among others. So, take a look at the schedule and contact me (425-488-3691, e-mail: dtallent@gtemail.net) if you can give us a hand at a meet.

I'd also like to thank the following people who will again this year be making a significant contribution to the WIOL program by helping at every meet: Debbie Newell, Don Denbo, Don Atkinson, and Jan Urban.

Also, since this will be my last year as Director, if you have a desire to be WIOL Director for 2000/2001 please let me know. We need you!

Dave Tallent, WIOL Director

Winter O’ Series Also Begins November 6

The 8th Winter O’ Series also begins on November 6. To participate in the Winter Series, all you have to do is show up at a designated meet (see the schedule) and run the longest course. You will receive points based on your order of finish (men and women are scored separately). Your best 4 scores are totaled, and the top 3 male and female finishers receive fabulous prizes (OK, they're really just chunks of marble). This year, we will also have a separate O’ Series Championship event, which will take place on February 26 at Hamlin Park.

Y2K Bash 2-Day Event

No, it's not a 2-day party, it's a 2-day meet with a party and night-o in between. This year's traditional “Bog Slog” will be even more fun. We're adding a second day of slogging, and at a new area – Lord Hill Park in Monroe.

Day 1 will take place on Friday, December 31 at the traditional location – St. Edward State Park in Kenmore. The evening's festivities include a potluck party and night-o at Rick and Eileen Breseman's home in Snohomish.

Day 2, on January 1, 2000, will feature the orienteering debut of Lord Hill Park in Monroe. Please plan to join us for one or both days of orienteering.

We're still working out the details of the meet, so keep checking the Cascade web site for additional information.

(Page 2)

COC Web Site

Cascade launched our redesigned web site in October. Featuring club-related events and information, it supplements the Pacific Northwest O' site. Webmaster Debbie Newell currently hosts the site on her personal account, but plans are underway to obtain a Cascade domain name, which will be easier to remember (hey, even Debbie can't remember the address). Look for details in future issues of Bearing 315 or on our web site, or call the Hotline (260-783-3866). Below is our current web address.

http://home1.gte.net/dnewel/Cascade/

OCAD 7 Opportunity

Cascade will be purchasing 2 club copies of OCAD 7 to be used for producing Cascade O' Club maps. Copies will be loaned out to anyone doing mapping for the club and, in compliance with the licensing agreement, must be removed from your computer when someone else uses the program.

You may also purchase your own copy of OCAD 7 at a 20% discount if you place your order when the club orders its copies. At current exchange rates, the program would cost about $320 with the discount. If you are interested in purchasing a copy of OCAD 7, contact Debbie Newell (425-488-3691, dnewel@gte.net) by November 30.

COC Annual Meeting, Dinner, and Election of Officers

Our annual meeting, dinner, and club elections will take place at Coco’s Restaurant in the Lake Forest Park Towne Centre upper level on November 14. We will, as always, spend as little time as possible on elections, and as much time as possible eating and socializing. Rick Hood will again come up with some clever orienteering games and Ardis Dull will show orienteering slides from her European trip, including the World O’ Champs in Scotland. There will also be a club board meeting preceding the annual meeting for those who want to attend.

Annual Meeting Schedule of Events

  • 4:00-5:00pm - Club board meeting
  • 5:00-6:15pm - Dinner (buy your own)
  • 6:15-6:30pm - Annual Meeting
  • 6:30-7:45pm - Entertainment

Mail-in Voting

Each year, at our annual meeting, we elect 5 members to the COC Board of Directors. These 5 members then select a President, Vice President, Secretary, and Treasurer. The President then appoints the club coordinators, and the whole bunch makes up the Board of Directors. All board meetings may be attended by anyone.

If you do not plan to attend the annual meeting, but still want to vote, you may mail or fax the ballot below. All ballots must be RECEIVED by November 13.

The nominating committee (Rick Hood, Rick Breseman, and Jim Siscel) is pleased to announce the nominees for the five (5) Elected Members of the COC 2000 Board of Directors.

Canoe-O (with foot-o’ option) - University of Washington - August 15

Meet Staff: Rick Hood, Don Atkinson, Harvey Friedman, Bill Cusworth

There were sunny skies the day before and the day after COC's annual Canoe-O. Unfortunately, during the event we had rain. (I think we were the only business the UW Waterfront Activity Center did that morning.)

We had an interesting and enjoyable format: any 5 controls in any order for the short course and any 10 controls in any order for the long course. Each control was marked on the map as “water access only,” “foot access only” or “either.” This gave those that didn’t want to rent a canoe the option of doing the short course, all from land.

Maybe even more unfortunate than the weather was the fact that some non-orienteers moved a water/land access control about 100 feet to a land access only. (Our guess is that these individuals sought some privacy and thought this would give them that. Of course, they didn’t know of the tenacity of orienteers, so rather than getting more privacy, they got more activity.) Because of this fact though, the times really don’t represent who did the best; thus we are listing all the hardy souls that competed in the short and long courses and awarding a first place finish to them all.

Overall, the format was fun and we will try it again. (Thanks to all those that came, in spite of the weather, and all those that helped.)

Bo Powell Memorial, Woodland Park - September 11

Meet Director: Don Atkinson
Course Designer: Harvey Friedman
Registration: Rick Hood, Ardis Dull
Starts: Bruce McAllister
Finishes: Bill Cusworth
Marker pickup: Harvey Friedman
Park permit: Dave Tallent

The weather was ideal, with sunny skies and temperatures near 70, for the first annual Bo Powell Memorial Orienteering meet, held at Woodland Park on September 11.

Bo Powell, one of the founders of the Cascade Orienteering Club, died last year. Len Englund, another of the club’s founders, urged the club to establish a meet in his memory, so this year the first annual Bo Powell Memorial Orienteering Meet was held at Woodland Park. Bo’s widow, Kathy, generously donated his compass to the club. This compass was used to create the Bo Powell Memorial Trophy, which will be presented to the fastest finisher on the long course each year. The recipient will have his/her name engraved on the trophy and pass it along to the next year’s winner. This year the trophy went to Cascade’s Eric Bone, who finished the 3.4k course in 18:36.

Many thanks to the meet staff and everyone who helped with beginner instruction and other tasks.

Beautiful weather brought a good crowd out to Fort Casey on Whidbey Island. The concrete gun batteries always provide a navigational challenge with their stairways and ladders, and this meet was no exception.

Course designer Paul Magnus set up a separate 1.2k course for beginners. Everyone else could choose between 1, 2, 3, or all 4 of the 2k loops, passing through the start/finish area to exchange maps after each.

Many thanks to Paul Magnus for setting courses and acquiring land permission.

block_index.text: [Image: The Cascade Orienteering Club logo: a square containing the line-art outline of a mountain range. Overlaid on the mountains is a compass rose with arrows pointing N, S, E, W.]
The newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club | November-December 1999 http://home1.gte.net/dnewel/Cascade/ | Page 1

The 18th Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League season begins on November 6 with a meet at Marymoor Park in Redmond. You don't have to be a student to attend a WIOL meet – all meets have courses for the public as well. So come out, support our WIOL program, and have some fun, too.
We already have course setters for ALL the WIOL/Winter O’ series meets:

I’d like to thank all the above for stepping forward at the beginning of the season, and making my job as WIOL Director a lot less stressful. But we still have a lot of other jobs that need to be done on meet days–registration, start and finish timing, and marker pickup, among others. So, take a look at the schedule and contact me (425-488-3691, e-mail: dtallent@gtemail.net) if you can give us a hand at a meet.

I'd also like to thank the following people who will again this year be making a significant contribution to the WIOL program by helping at every meet: Debbie Newell, Don Denbo, Don Atkinson, and Jan Urban.
Also, since this will be my last year as Director, if you have a desire to be WIOL Director for 2000/2001 please let me know. We need you!
Winter O’ Series Also Begins November 6

The 8th Winter O’ Series also begins on November 6. To participate in the Winter Series, all you have to do is show up at a designated meet (see the schedule) and run the longest course. You will receive points based on your order of finish (men and women are scored separately). Your best 4 scores are totaled, and the top 3 male and female finishers receive fabulous prizes (OK, they're really just chunks of marble). This year, we will also have a separate O’ Series Championship event, which will take place on February 26 at Hamlin Park.

No, it's not a 2-day party, it's a 2-day meet with a party and night-o in between. This year's traditional “Bog Slog” will be even more fun. We're adding a second day of slogging, and at a new area – Lord Hill Park in Monroe.

Day 1 will take place on Friday, December 31 at the traditional location – St. Edward State Park in Kenmore. The evening's festivities include a potluck party and night-o at Rick and Eileen Breseman's home in Snohomish.
Day 2, on January 1, 2000, will feature the orienteering debut of Lord Hill Park in Monroe. Please plan to join us for one or both days of orienteering.
We're still working out the details of the meet, so keep checking the Cascade web site for additional information.
COC Annual Meeting, Dinner, and Club Elections .......................................... 2

Meet results Big St. Denny Goat - March 6 ................................3 (you didn't think you'd ever see them, did you?) Fin Hill Trail Adventure - June 21 ..........................3 UW Canoe-O - August 15 .......................................3 Bo Powell Memorial - September 11 .....................3 Inland NW O' Cup - September 18-19....................4 NW results at US Champs - September 25-26 .......5 Lake Sammamish State Park - October 3 ..............6 Fort Casey State Park - October 9..........................6 Hamlin Park Vampire O' - October 24 ...................6

Cascade launched our redesigned web site in October. Featuring club-related events and information, it supplements the Pacific Northwest O' site. Webmaster Debbie Newell currently hosts the site on her personal account, but plans are underway to obtain a Cascade domain name, which will be easier to remember (hey, even Debbie can't remember the address). Look for details in future issues of Bearing 315 or on our web site, or call the Hotline (260-783-3866). Below is our current web address.

Cascade will be purchasing 2 club copies of OCAD 7 to be used for producing Cascade O' Club maps. Copies will be loaned out to anyone doing mapping for the club and, in compliance with the licensing agreement, must be removed from your computer when someone else uses the program.

You may also purchase your own copy of OCAD 7 at a 20% discount if you place your order when the club orders its copies. At current exchange rates, the program would cost about $320 with the discount. If you are interested in purchasing a copy of OCAD 7, contact Debbie Newell (425-488-3691, dnewel@gte.net) by November 30.
COC Annual Meeting, Dinner, and Election of Officers

Our annual meeting, dinner, and club elections will take place at Coco’s Restaurant in the Lake Forest Park Towne Centre upper level on November 14. We will, as always, spend as little time as possible on elections, and as much time as possible eating and socializing. Rick Hood will again come up with some clever orienteering games and Ardis Dull will show orienteering slides from her European trip, including the World O’ Champs in Scotland. There will also be a club board meeting preceding the annual meeting for those who want to attend.

Each year, at our annual meeting, we elect 5 members to the COC Board of Directors. These 5 members then select a President, Vice President, Secretary, and Treasurer. The President then appoints the club coordinators, and the whole bunch makes up the Board of Directors. All board meetings may be attended by anyone.
If you do not plan to attend the annual meeting, but still want to vote, you may mail or fax the ballot below. All ballots must be RECEIVED by November 13.
The nominating committee (Rick Hood, Rick Breseman, and Jim Siscel) is pleased to announce the nominees for the five (5) Elected Members of the COC 2000 Board of Directors.

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ ┃ BALLOT ┃ ┃ [ ] Don Atkinson ┃ [ ] Eric Bone ┃ [ ] Debbie Newell ┃ [ ] Dave Tallent ┃ [ ] Jan Urban ┃ ┃ [ ] Accept all 5 named candidates ┃ ┃ [ ] Write-in name__________________________ ┃ ┃ I certify by my signature below that I am a ┃ member in good standing (as of November 10) of ┃ the Cascade Orienteering Club. ┃ ┃ Signed________________________ Date__________ ┗━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┛

Place a check in the box next to each candidate you wish to vote for. Vote for a total of 5 individuals (any write-in candidate must be a COC member in good standing as of November 10, 1999 and must have agreed to serve if elected).
Mail or fax the ballot to: Ballots c/o Rick Hood 22631 87th Pl W Edmonds WA 98026 fax: 206-374-2619
Newsletter Editor – vacant (Debbie Newell temporary editor)

I’m sorry if I forgot anyone. Thanks to all the volunteers. —Eric Bone
Long Goat (10.8 km, 515 m climb, 23 c)

BEHS - Burlington-Edison High School Naval Junior ROTC
Sammamish O' Club Meet Director: Bruce McAlister Courses: Taavi Tatsi
Canoe-O (with foot-o’ option) - University of Washington - August 15
Meet Staff: Rick Hood, Don Atkinson, Harvey Friedman, Bill Cusworth

There were sunny skies the day before and the day after COC's annual Canoe-O. Unfortunately, during the event we had rain. (I think we were the only business the UW Waterfront Activity Center did that morning.)

We had an interesting and enjoyable format: any 5 controls in any order for the short course and any 10 controls in any order for the long course. Each control was marked on the map as “water access only,” “foot access only” or “either.” This gave those that didn’t want to rent a canoe the option of doing the short course, all from land.

Maybe even more unfortunate than the weather was the fact that some non-orienteers moved a water/land access control about 100 feet to a land access only. (Our guess is that these individuals sought some privacy and thought this would give them that. Of course, they didn’t know of the tenacity of orienteers, so rather than getting more privacy, they got more activity.) Because of this fact though, the times really don’t represent who did the best; thus we are listing all the hardy souls that competed in the short and long courses and awarding a first place finish to them all.
Overall, the format was fun and we will try it again. (Thanks to all those that came, in spite of the weather, and all those that helped.)

Long Course — Bob and Bobby Seidensticker Forgrave Family Max Wells and Clan Will and Val Enger Alex Izzo & Sona Zaveri Molly, Madaline, and Dave Enger Carol Vierck and team Michael Pope and crew Bob Kendall
Short Course — Dave Petty Mike Spero & crew
Bo Powell Memorial, Woodland Park - September 11

Meet Director: Don Atkinson Course Designer: Harvey Friedman Registration: Rick Hood, Ardis Dull Starts: Bruce McAllister Finishes: Bill Cusworth Marker pickup: Harvey Friedman Park permit: Dave Tallent
The weather was ideal, with sunny skies and temperatures near 70, for the first annual Bo Powell Memorial Orienteering meet, held at Woodland Park on September 11.

Bo Powell, one of the founders of the Cascade Orienteering Club, died last year. Len Englund, another of the club’s founders, urged the club to establish a meet in his memory, so this year the first annual Bo Powell Memorial Orienteering Meet was held at Woodland Park. Bo’s widow, Kathy, generously donated his compass to the club. This compass was used to create the Bo Powell Memorial Trophy, which will be presented to the fastest finisher on the long course each year. The recipient will have his/her name engraved on the trophy and pass it along to the next year’s winner. This year the trophy went to Cascade’s Eric Bone, who finished the 3.4k course in 18:36.
Many thanks to the meet staff and everyone who helped with beginner instruction and other tasks.
Meet Director: John Beck Course Design: Bill Borrie
